1984.a: Ethics: doing right by participants. GDPR: legal data protection. Both required.

1985.q: Purely technical thesis no human data?
1985.a: Possible. Methodology focuses on technical implementation, testing, evaluation.

1986.a: 200-300 words: purpose, methods, key results, conclusions. Written last, placed first.

1987.a: Specific, answerable, related to problem. Usually 1-3 main questions.

1988.a: Narrow focus. Define specific aspect. Add constraints (context, time, scope).

1989.a: Define what's included and excluded. Justify boundaries. Prevents scope creep.

1990.a: Introduction, literature review, methodology, implementation, results, discussion, conclusions.

1991.a: Key theories and concepts underlying your research. Foundation from literature.

1992.a: Describe research approach, data collection, analysis methods, ethical considerations.

1993.a: Results: present findings objectively. Discussion: interpret, compare, implications.

1994.a: Present data first without interpretation. Then discuss meaning in separate section.

1995.a: Acknowledge weaknesses transparently. Shows academic maturity. Suggest how to address.

1996.a: Yes, directly address each research question. Summarize key findings.

1997.a: Practical suggestions based on findings. Specific, actionable, for identified audience.

1998.a: Each recommendation should be supported by your findings. Reference specific results.

1999.a: APA style. In-text: (Author, Year). Full reference list at end. Consistent formatting.

2000.a: Peer-reviewed journals, books, official reports. Blogs/Wikipedia NOT acceptable as primary sources.

2001.a: Minimum 10-20 for bachelor's thesis. More is better. Quality over quantity.

2002.a: Library databases (Finna), Google Scholar, IEEE, ACM, ProQuest. Ask librarian.

2003.a: Finna, ProQuest, EBSCO, IEEE Xplore, ACM Digital Library. Library provides access.

2004.a: Install tool, browser plugin, Word plugin. Collect sources as you read. Generate bibliography.

2005.a: Good for finding sources. Cross-reference with library databases for full text access.

2006.a: Use reference management tool. Double-check in-text matches bibliography. Be consistent.

2007.a: Set weekly writing goals. Protected writing time. Small consistent progress.

2008.a: Set page/word count targets. E.g., 500 words/day or 1 chapter/2 weeks.

2009.a: Break into small tasks. Set deadlines. Write imperfect drafts first. Join writing groups.

2010.a: Read carefully. Implement changes. Ask clarification if unclear. Track revisions.

2011.a: Send polite follow-up after 1-2 weeks. Be patient. Plan timeline with buffer.

2012.a: Monthly minimum. More frequently during intensive writing periods. Agree schedule early.

2013.a: Programme assigns new supervisor. Your work continues. Contact programme coordinator.

2014.a: Contact programme coordinator. Must have valid reason. Not guaranteed.

2015.a: Discuss professionally. Present your reasoning. Supervisor guides but you own the work.

2016.a: Check programme policy. If allowed, disclose use. AI assists but you own the content.

2017.a: State which tools used, for what purpose, in methodology or acknowledgments section.

2018.a: May be acceptable for non-native speakers. Disclose. Final text is your responsibility.

2019.a: Yes, for English programmes. Must match programme language.

2020.q: Write thesis in Finnish in English programme?
2020.a: Check with supervisor. Usually thesis language matches programme language.

2021.a: Academic Finnish proficiency needed. Not suitable for beginners.

2022.a: Not standard. One language throughout. Abstract in both Finnish and English.

2023.a: Yes, present in thesis seminar. Not a formal defense like PhD. Q&A from peers and supervisor.

2024.a: Background, research questions, methods, key findings, conclusions, 15-20 minutes.

2025.a: Semi-formal. Professional but supportive atmosphere. Academic discussion style.

2026.a: Know your work thoroughly. Anticipate questions about methodology and limitations.

2027.a: Receive feedback. Revise and present again. Part of iterative process.

2028.a: Revise based on feedback. Resubmit. Not a final rejection, part of the process.

2029.a: 2-3 revision rounds typical. Some theses need more. Plan time accordingly.

2030.a: 2-4 weeks after final submission. Supervisor and second reader assess.

2031.a: Contact Study Services. Ask supervisor for timeline. Priority grading may be possible.

2032.a: Contact supervisor and Study Services with justification (job start, permit deadline).

2033.a: Study certificate showing expected graduation. Contact Study Services.

2034.a: Follow Laurea thesis template: margins, fonts, spacing, headings all specified.

2035.a: Available on student intranet. Word template with pre-set styles.

2036.a: Sequential numbering (Figure 1, Table 1). Caption below figures, above tables. Reference in text.

2037.a: Source credited below figure. If your own, state 'Author's own'. Permission for copyrighted images.

2038.a: In text for key algorithms. Full code in appendices. Use code formatting.

2039.a: Yes, in appendices or methodology. Provides access to full implementation.

2040.q: Submit as web app or PDF?
2040.a: PDF for thesis document. Web app as separate deliverable linked in thesis.

2041.a: Thesis + product documentation (user guide, technical docs, deployment guide).

2042.a: In appendices. Screenshots, step-by-step instructions, system requirements.

2043.a: Anonymize vulnerabilities. Responsible disclosure principles. Discuss with supervisor.

2044.a: Responsible disclosure. Don't publish active vulnerabilities. Discuss with supervisor and client.

2045.a: Document all licenses used. Ensure compliance. Include license information.

2046.a: Possible with programme approval. Each member must show individual contribution.

2047.a: Individual grades based on individual contribution, shared work, and personal reflection.

2048.a: Document contributions. Inform supervisor. Individual grading protects active contributors.

2049.a: Possible. Discuss with supervisor. May need to adjust scope significantly.

2050.a: Cite your own previous work. Rephrase and add new analysis. Self-citation required.

2051.a: Reusing own previous text without citation. Must cite own work and add new content.

2052.a: Must cite. Can't copy-paste. Rephrase and extend with new analysis.

2053.a: Must cite and substantially revise. Add new literature. Don't just copy.

2055.a: Through designated system on student intranet. Final PDF after all approvals.

2056.a: PDF. Follow naming convention: LastName_FirstName_Thesis_Year.pdf. Check requirements.

2057.a: Contact Study Services immediately. Email thesis as backup. Screenshot error.

2058.a: Screenshot of error, email timestamp, browser history. Contact Study Services.

2059.a: Contact supervisor for extension. Late submission may delay graduation.

2060.a: Written appeal within 14 days. Include specific reasoning. Follow Degree Regulations.

2061.a: Yes, typically 30 ECTS (~800 hours). Required for graduation. Check your curriculum.

2062.a: Work that develops professional competences aligned with degree learning outcomes.

2063.a: Yes, if tasks align with outcomes. Total hours must meet requirements. Longer duration.

2064.a: Yes, through RPL/AHOT if relevant to degree outcomes. Provide documentation.

2065.a: Possible if learning outcomes met. Apply for RPL with documentation of relevant tasks.

2066.a: Must relate to degree field. IT student: development, analysis, project work. Not just any job.

2067.a: Generally no for BIT-specific internship credits. May count for general electives.

2068.a: Possibly through RPL/AHOT for transferable skills. Discuss with tutor teacher.

2069.a: Connect your tasks to specific course learning outcomes. Show what competences you gained.

2070.a: Specific, measurable goals aligned with degree outcomes. What you will learn and develop.

2071.a: Internship coordinator or tutor teacher reviews and approves placement and objectives.

2072.a: Yes, three-party agreement (student, employer, Laurea) should be signed before starting.

2073.a: Discuss benefits with employer. Contact internship coordinator for help. May need different employer.

2074.a: Difficult. Agreement is normally required. Contact Study Services about exceptions.

2075.q: Start job then want it to count?
2075.a: Possible retroactively through RPL. Documentation and supervisor confirmation needed.

2076.a: Internship report, timesheets/hours log, employer evaluation, learning agreement.

2077.a: Yes, log of hours worked. Shows you met the required total hours.

2078.a: Yes, employer assessment of your performance and competence development.

2079.a: Provide simple evaluation form. Offer to draft and have them review/sign.

2080.a: Can supplement but don't replace official timesheet. Show employment verification.

2081.a: ~800 hours for 30 ECTS. 1 ECTS = ~27 hours. Check programme requirements.

2082.a: Possible with coordinator approval. Combined hours must meet requirements.

2083.a: Yes, internship can span multiple periods if total hours met.

2084.a: Yes, encouraged. Erasmus+ traineeship funding available. Contact International Services.

2085.a: May be accepted. Discuss with coordinator. Must demonstrate learning outcomes.

2086.a: Anonymize company data. Focus on your learning and competence development.

2087.a: Use pseudonyms. Remove identifying details. Focus on your role and learning.

2088.a: Introduction, company description, tasks, learning outcomes, reflection, conclusions.

2089.a: Link your practical experience to theoretical concepts from your courses.

2090.a: Typically Pass/Fail or 0-5. Based on report, employer feedback, learning outcomes.

2091.a: Many programmes use Pass/Fail. Check your programme's assessment method.

2092.a: Discuss with coordinator. Poor feedback doesn't auto-fail. Learning reflection matters.

2093.a: Contact coordinator. New agreement needed. Already completed hours may count.

2094.a: Possible if enough hours for partial credit. Discuss with coordinator.

2095.a: Completed hours still count. May need new placement for remaining hours.

2096.a: Stop internship. Report to coordinator. Safety first. New placement arranged.

2097.a: Coordinator can mediate between student and employer. Contact them for help.

2098.a: Report to coordinator. Finnish labor laws protect even interns. TE-services can advise.

2099.a: Check Finnish labor law. Unpaid internship must be primarily educational. Many are paid.

2100.a: Plan schedule carefully. Some flexibility may be possible. Discuss with both coordinator and employer.

2101.a: Generally no. Internship has specific requirements. Contact Study Services about exceptions.

2102.a: May be possible if it meets learning outcomes. Apply through RPL. Discuss with coordinator.

2103.a: Possible if tasks align with degree outcomes. Requires agreement and approval.

2104.a: Difficult. Must be structured with learning objectives. Discuss with coordinator.

2105.a: Through RPL/AHOT for elective credits. Document participation and learning.

2106.a: Recognition of Prior Learning. Get credits for existing competences from work, study, or life.

2107.a: Apply early in studies. Saves time by avoiding courses where you already have competence.

2108.a: Certificates, portfolio, work references, detailed competence descriptions, samples.

2109.a: Yes, demonstrates technical competences. Include README descriptions of projects.

2110.a: Yes, with client references or project documentation. Show relevant competences.

2111.a: Yes, with certificates and course content descriptions. Must match learning outcomes.

2112.a: Map your competences to specific learning outcomes of the course you want to replace.

2113.a: May need translation or summary in English/Finnish. Check with Study Services.

2114.a: Official translations may be needed for formal documents. Self-translation may suffice for descriptions.

2115.a: A few weeks typically. Apply early to plan studies accordingly.

2116.a: Can resubmit with additional evidence. Discuss feedback with coordinator.

2117.a: Possible. May need to complete remaining parts. Discussed case by case.

2118.a: Yes, teacher may request demonstration (exam, presentation) instead of automatic approval.

2119.a: Based on same rubric/outcomes as the course. Shows you can meet all requirements.

2120.a: Format varies: could be project, exam, presentation, or combination. Teacher decides.

2121.a: Possible. Teacher may want to verify competence through both.

2122.a: Review course learning outcomes. Prepare evidence. Practice presentation. Know the material.

2123.a: Same rubric as course assessment. Based on learning outcomes.

2124.a: Revise and retry, or take the full course. Discuss with teacher.

2125.a: Usually one retry allowed. Check with teacher and Study Services.

2126.a: AHOT credits often recorded as Pass (no numeric grade), not affecting GPA.

2127.a: Yes, may be marked as 'RPL' or 'Hyväksiluettu' on transcript.

2128.a: May be visible on transcript. Most employers don't differentiate.

2129.a: Possible with appropriate language certificates or proficiency evidence.

2130.a: Possible with evidence of relevant professional communication experience.

2131.a: Possible with evidence (university transcripts, certifications, demonstrated competence).

2132.a: Through work experience RPL. Document relevant professional experience.

2133.a: Thesis seminar may not be AHOT-able. Research methods course may be. Check with coordinator.

2134.a: Yes, fewer courses to take means potentially earlier graduation.

2135.a: Can apply while enrolled. If approved, drop the course.

2136.a: Drop the course enrollment to free up your schedule.

2137.a: Generally no fees for degree students. Check with Study Services.

2138.a: Your tutor teacher or programme coordinator handles AHOT assessments.

2139.a: Insufficient evidence, competences don't match outcomes, outdated experience.

2140.a: Detailed outcome mapping, specific examples, certificates, portfolio, references.

2141.a: Start small. Ask one question per class. Preparation helps confidence. Practice improves it.

2142.a: Read academic texts regularly. Keep vocabulary notebook. Use context clues.

2143.a: Exposure helps. Ask for repetition politely. Finnish accent is generally clear.

2144.a: Very normal in Finland. Say 'Could you repeat that?' or 'Could you explain differently?'

2145.a: Build glossary for each course. Ask teacher to explain terms. Use course materials.

2146.a: Yes, clear simple English is fine. Content matters more than fancy language.

2147.a: Content weighted more than language. But clear communication expected. Use Grammarly.

2148.a: Primarily content. Language clarity is important but not the main criterion.

2149.a: Yes, through library workshops, wellbeing services, and teacher feedback.

2150.a: Check student intranet. Some programmes offer writing support or peer writing groups.

2151.a: Topic sentence, supporting evidence, analysis, concluding link. One idea per paragraph.

2152.a: Introduction: context, purpose, structure. Conclusion: key findings, implications, recommendations.

2153.a: No contractions, no slang, no first person (unless reflection), evidence-based statements.

2154.a: Don't just describe. Evaluate, compare, question. Why? So what? What does it mean?

2155.a: (Author, Year) for paraphrase. (Author, Year, p. X) for direct quote. Every claim needs source.

2156.a: Cite while writing, not after. Use reference management tool. When in doubt, cite.

2157.a: Save source immediately when reading. Use Zotero/Mendeley. Note page numbers.

2158.a: Read, close source, write in own words, then verify. Use your own sentence structure.

2159.a: Read abstract first, then conclusions, then introduction. Skim methods. Focus on findings.

2160.a: Abstract > headings > figures > conclusion. Read fully only if relevant.

2161.a: Last 5-10 years preferred. Use academic databases. Check citation count.

2162.a: Check if indexed in major databases. Look for peer review process. Beek's list helpful.

2163.a: Start with Finna. Filter by peer-reviewed. Use simple keywords. Ask librarian for help.

2164.a: Use key concepts from your topic. Try synonyms. Use Boolean operators (AND, OR, NOT).

2165.a: Add filters: date, peer-reviewed, language, subject area. Narrow keywords.

2166.a: Author credentials, publication venue, peer review, date, citations by others, methodology.

2167.a: Organize by themes, not source by source. Show connections between sources. Identify gaps.

2168.a: Compare findings across sources. Identify patterns, agreements, disagreements. Build argument.

2169.a: Don't string quotes together. Use your own voice. Sources support YOUR argument.

2170.a: Choose appropriate chart type. Label axes. Include units. Reference figure in text.

2171.a: State what the chart shows, highlight key patterns, interpret significance.

2172.a: State approach, justify choices, describe steps, explain data collection and analysis.

2173.a: Use measured language. 'The results suggest' not 'The results prove.' State limitations.

2174.a: Frame positively: 'Future research could...' Show awareness, not weakness.

2175.a: Practice aloud multiple times. Time yourself. Know key points without reading.

2176.a: Listen to academic talks. Practice key terms. Record yourself. Focus on clarity, not accent.

2177.a: Ask to repeat or rephrase. 'Could you clarify your question?' is perfectly acceptable.

2178.a: Completely normal: 'Could you rephrase that?' or 'Do you mean...?'

2179.a: Take your time. Pause to think. Short clear answers are fine. Quality over speed.

2180.a: Yes, note cards are fine. Don't read full sentences. Key points and data only.

2181.a: Reading directly usually lowers presentation grade. Use slides as prompts, not scripts.

2182.a: Max 6 words per bullet. Use images, diagrams, data. Slides support speech, don't replace it.

2183.a: Practice with timer. Know which parts to cut if running long. Watch clock during presentation.

2184.a: Divide parts by comfort. Support weaker speakers. Practice together. Focus on teamwork.

2185.q: Ensure fairness when fluent member speaks most?
2185.a: Equal presentation time. Assign specific sections. Each member presents their own work.

2186.a: By content expertise, not language ability. Each person presents their contribution.

2187.a: One editor reviews for consistency. Each writes their section. Collaborative editing.

2188.a: Focus on clarity, not criticism. Suggest improvements. Use tracked changes. Be kind.

2189.a: Direct but polite. Respect personal space. On time is important. Less small talk than some cultures.

2190.a: Finns may be quiet but engaged. Don't interpret silence as disagreement. Ask directly.

2191.q: Follow up when no quick reply?
2191.a: Wait 2-3 days. Send polite reminder. Finns aren't ignoring you, may be busy.

2192.a: Be specific and concise. State what you need. Include deadline. One topic per message.

2193.a: Be explicit. Avoid sarcasm in text. Use emojis sparingly for tone. Re-read before sending.

2194.a: Focus on issues, not persons. Use 'I feel' statements. Seek mediation if needed.

2195.a: Ask to use English. Join actively. Suggest English as working language in group agreement.

2196.a: 'Could we use English so everyone can participate?' Finns generally accommodate willingly.

2197.a: Show through code, designs, documentation. Written contributions demonstrate competence.

2198.a: Document your work (commits, screenshots, task boards). Written evidence speaks clearly.

2199.a: Set small goals. Celebrate progress. Use support services. Connect with peers. It gets easier.

2200.a: Check email/Canvas daily. Review notes same day. Set weekly goals. Sleep well. Ask for help early.
